The Story of My Steps: A Visual Journey Through Daily Walking Habits

{js, echo=FALSE} // Progressive reveal JavaScript - DO NOT REMOVE OR EDIT (function() { document.querySelectorAll(‘h2’).forEach(function(el) { el.style.opacity = ‘0’; el.style.transform = ‘translateY(20px)’; el.style.transition = ‘opacity 0.6s ease, transform 0.6s ease’; });

document.querySelectorAll(‘h2 ~ p, h2 ~ img, h2 ~ ul, h2 ~ ol’).forEach(function(el) { el.style.opacity = ‘0’; el.style.transform = ‘translateY(20px)’; el.style.transition = ‘opacity 0.6s ease, transform 0.6s ease’; });

window.addEventListener(‘scroll’, function() { document.querySelectorAll(‘h2’).forEach(function(el) { var rect = el.getBoundingClientRect(); if (rect.top < window.innerHeight * 0.85) { el.style.opacity = ‘1’; el.style.transform = ‘translateY(0)’; } }); }); })(); ``` ##The Data Behind My Walks Every day, I step outside and walk. Sometimes it’s a quick 10-minute stroll between classes. Other times, it’s a full hour wandering with friends. For this project, I logged 40 walks over several weeks, recording the duration of each walk, who accompanied me, and my pace. The goal was to uncover patterns in my walking behaviour that I might not have noticed otherwise.

The data was collected using a Google Form that I completed immediately after each walk. This real-time logging ensured accuracy. The form captures timestamps automatically, giving me a precise record of when each walk happened.

##When Do I Walk Most? This first visualization reveals my walking patterns across the week. The data shows which days I’m most active, based on timestamps automatically recorded by Google Forms.

Bar chart showing walks by day of week

The bar chart shows a clear pattern: my walking is not evenly distributed across the week. This makes sense when I think about my schedule. The visualization uses a clean green tone from my Set2 inspired colour palette.

##Does Company Change My Pace? Walking alone versus walking with others creates very different experiences. This visualization explores whether my pace changes depending on who I’m with.

Faceted bar chart of pace by companion

The faceted bar chart breaks down pace categories for each companion type. When I walk alone, I tend to move at a brisker pace. Walking with friends introduces a more relaxed rhythm, with conversation naturally slowing things down.

##Morning Rush or Evening Stroll? Time of day affects everything. This final visualization examines average walk duration across different times of day.

Column chart of walk duration by time of day

The column chart reveals a compelling story: my walks get progressively longer as the day goes on. Morning walks tend to be quick and purposeful. Evening walks stretch out, becoming more leisurely.

What These Patterns Tell Me Looking at these three visualizations together, a coherent story emerges. My walking habits reflect the rhythms of daily life. The most interesting insight is the evening effect. Walks become longer as the day progresses.

Understanding these patterns has practical value. Data turns unconscious habits into intentional choices.

All data collected personally using Google Forms. Visualizations created with R and ggplot2.
